name: Hardened libc++ / MSVC STL / libstdc++
summary: The clearest normative C++26 standard-library hardening: bounds and precondition checks in standard containers and iterators with controlled performance overhead. libc++ shipped 2024; MSVC STL ships an opt-in mode (VS 2022 17.14, May 2025); libstdc++ is actively implementing.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hardened libc++ / MSVC STL / libstdc++ is the clearest normative C++26 standard-library hardening / bounds-checking memory-safety addition: bounds/precondition checks in standard containers and iterators with controlled performance overhead. libc++ uses `_LIBCPP_HARDENING_MODE` ∈ {`none`, `fast`, `extensive`, `debug`}; `_LIBCPP_ABI_BOUNDED_ITERATORS` makes iterator types carry bounds for `span`/`string_view`. Shipped 2024.

MSVC STL ships `_MSVC_STL_HARDENING=1` opt-in (VS 2022 17.14, May 2025); uses `__fastfail`/`__builtin_verbose_trap` for ~29-byte trap codegen; default in a future release. libstdc++ is actively implementing.

sections.title: Rollout and significance
sections.content:
Google reports significant CVE-class elimination from rolling out `fast` mode in production. Standardized in C++26; vendor-shipping but mostly opt-in. The pragmatic C++ safety story is: Profiles for the language layer is unfinished; library hardening is what's actually shipping.

The hardening modes ladder from `none` (no checks) through `fast` (production-grade preconditions on container access, predictable cost) to `extensive` and `debug` (more checks, higher cost). The trap codegen in MSVC's implementation is deliberately compact (~29 bytes) so that hardened code remains close in size to unchecked code.
